data.title: The Tardy September Jobs Report Shows Overall Weakness
data.description:
NEW YORK (November 20): The Bureau of Labor Statistics ('BLS') printed 119,000 new jobs according to the Establishment Survey, a collection of job creation data from businesses replying to the survey. The survey results had been long delayed by the government shutdown. We expect there will be considerable revisions to the report given the delay.

The number was well above the consensus estimate of 50,000 but tempered considerably by a poor three-month average of new job creation of just 62,300, well below par. The private payroll number was 97,000 after taking account of 22,000 government jobs added. Revisions resulted in 33,000 fewer jobs for July and August.

The BLS’s Household Survey, which polls the number of people taking jobs and is viewed as eliminating workers holding more than one job, printed well above the Establishment Survey at 225,000 new jobs.

The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% from 4.3%, and the U6 rate tipped down from 8.1% to 8.0%. The “U6” is the percentage of the population that is unemployed, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force, plus total employed part-time for economic reasons, plus all persons marginally attached to the labor force. Some 436,000 people joined the labor force, pushing the Labor Force Participation Rate up slightly, to 62.3% from 62.2% in July. We note that 708,000 foreign-born people, which includes citizens, visa holders, and illegal aliens, have left the workforce since last September.

Let’s look at our exclusive schedule of August and September Jobs Creation by Average Weekly Wages:

Jobs creation was led, once again, by what we call “government-adjacent” jobs: Private Education & Health Services and Healthcare and Social Services, which tend to be funded by governmental programs, followed by Leisure & Hospitality. Construction gained 20,000 jobs, but the Wholesale Trade and Business & Professional services both lost jobs.

We do not include “official” government jobs payrolls in our chart, but government gained 22,000 jobs.

As this is the September jobs report, we also show here the real wage gains or losses for the month, based on the Trimmed Mean average inflation rate, as below.

Economy Generally

Today’s numbers add to our recession concerns that had troubled us going back to January, although they had been assuaged in the earlier part of the year.

As we explained back in January, historically, a recession is almost assured after the 3-month/10-year inverted yield curve is resolved. That had happened at the end of 2024.

The data of late shows the economy is slowing significantly, and there will likely be a considerable negative effect on Q4 from the shutdown. First quarter GDP printed basically down at -0.5% in the third and final estimate. But GDP minus elements of “noise,” and formally referred to as "Real final sales to private domestic purchasers" - printed at 1.9% in the final print. For 2025Q2, GDP printed at 3.8% in the Final Estimate released September 25th. “Real final sales to private domestic purchasers” printed at just 2.9%. But as explained in our Q2 GDP report, the GDP percentage data is illusory because of the anomaly in 2025Q1 Net Export data.

We have thought for some time that the threat to the economy from the tariffs is considerably overblown and largely driven by media hand-wringing. We continue to believe that seller margins, not revenue, will be affected by President Trump’s tariffs, as we explained in this article from last November. As we explained just recently here, the main drivers of inflation are coming from services, not goods subject to tariffs, and from the decline in the US Dollar Index.

Nevertheless, we continue to believe the ongoing collapse of China’s manufacturing economy we discussed here will cause China’s prices for manufactured goods to fall so that the cost of Chinese goods, after tariffs, will be within a couple of standard deviations of the 2024 average prices to American consumers. Walmart (WMT), and we expect a number of other large US importers, will use the buyers market to force their wholesale price from Chinese manufacturers down considerably.

The public also thinks inflation expectations also seem to be overblown, with the 5-year inflation expectation at 2.18, down 14 bps from 2.32 when Trump was sworn in, but up around 16 bps from where it was in April, when President Trump's tariff policy was first announced. It seems the public has adapted to a lower rate after recent tariff agreements.

Nevertheless, it’s clear the Fed has botched rates, which we believe should be 50 to 75 bps lower than they are now and on course to be down 100 bps by year-end.

Prognostications

We’re largely “flying blind,” given the dearth of data since the shutdown. What we have points to a slowing economy. However, as we said in our 2025Q2 GDP report, we think there may be substantially increased capex owing to the Big Beautiful Bill. However, that won’t be reflected, likely, until the later part of 2026Q3 or even Q4. Unfortunately for the Republicans, that will likely cause the House to flip to the Democrats in the 2026 midterm elections. We expect 2025 Q3 to print at around 1.5%, +/- 25 bps. The release is not scheduled until December 19th due to the shutdown. There will not be a jobs report for October; instead, the October report will be printed as part of the November report on December 16th. Given the dearth of data, we will not offer a prognostication on 2025Q4 GDP until we see the October/November jobs report.

Other Data Points

Ordinarily, we provide other data points that we use to assess the overall standing of the economy. Inasmuch as most of that data is provided by the government and was not provided due to the government shutdown, we will forego it for this September jobs report. We will return to it in our next jobs report.

_____________________________________

NOTE: Our commentaries most often tend to be event-driven. They are mostly written from a public policy, economic, or political/geopolitical perspective. Some are written from a management consulting perspective for companies that we believe to be underperforming. While we have never written about any companies that are our clients and do not include companies we write about here in our personal investment portfolios, we do provide here strategies that we would have recommended were the companies our clients. Others discuss new management strategies of non-client companies we believe will fail. This approach lends special value to contrarian investors to uncover potential opportunities in companies that are otherwise in a downturn. (Opinions regarding such companies here, however, assume the company will not change.)

data.title: Dollarama: Ringing Up Too Big A Tab, There's No Bargain At This Price
data.description:
Introduction

Dollarama Inc. (DOL:CA) has been a name I've had mixed feelings on for some time now. Back in February, I noted that the company was executing well and still had a good runway for growth, the valuation was a bit stretched at 30x earnings, trading well above peers and at the high end of the historical range. Fast-forward nine months, and the company has continued to surpass expectations on the business front, pushing shares higher by 41% over this time period.

While I acknowledge that the company is making good progress on its goals to get to 2,200 stores in Canada by 2034, the valuation has stretched further to a whopping 42x forward earnings. In this article, I'll outline why, despite the impressive business performance, there are risks present in the stock like lower inflation, a shakier Canadian consumer, and lofty valuation that will make it difficult for investors to achieve above-market returns unless the company performs remarkably ahead of expectations.

Recent Results

Dollarama has been firing on all cylinders lately. During its most recent quarter for Q2’26, the company reported a top and bottom-line beat, with revenues of $1.72 billion surpassing estimates by $25 million and EPS of $1.16 coming in a penny ahead of expectations.

When we drill down into the drivers of the company’s recent performance, revenues increased 10.3% year-over-year, thanks to 4.9% same-store sales growth in Canada and a higher store count. The drivers of the same store sales growth saw a 3.9% rise in transactions and a 0.9% lift in average basket size, which was to be expected as inflation normalizes. Consumables were a category leader, while general merchandise and seasonal categories were stable.

Moving down the income statement, gross profit dollars clocked in at $784.5 million for the quarter, with margins expanding 30bps to 45.5% as lower trucking costs helped take the pressure of margins. Unfortunately, some of this was partially offset by higher SG&A costs as a percentage of revenue, coming in 40bps higher to 14.0%. Netting these two effects together, EBITDA of $588.5 million came in 11.6% higher than last year, with EPS slightly higher at 13.7% thanks to a slightly lower share count.

Since last year’s Q3, Dollarama has reduced its share count by 2.9 million shares, with about 932k shares being repurchased in the latest quarter alone for a total amount of $174.8 million. Overall, I’m really not a fan of the share repurchases, as the company is buying back shares at more than a 30x free cash flow multiple. Rather, I’d like to see the company deploy more capital on increasing the store count and growing organically that way.

To that end, one of the things that’s been driving some of the store count was Dollarcity opening its first store in the Mexican market, with a plan for five more stores by year-end. As a reminder, Dollarcity is Dollarama’s Latin American exposure with a total network sitting at about 658 stores, having added 14 stores during the quarter.

Another place cash flow has gone is acquisitions. In March of this year, Dollarama bought The Reject Shop, which is Australia’s largest discount retailer and brings some $880 million of annual revenue after paying $233 million for the entire business for an 8.9x EBITDA multiple. That’s a much better valuation and use of capital compared to Dollarama buying its own shares in the open market at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 23x.

While The Reject Shop isn’t a growth business like Dollarama, it does give it a presence in a new geography and expands the footprint of the network. Back home, the Canadian business added 27 net new stores, bringing the total to 1,665, and remains firmly on track for the 70-80 net new openings this year.

As for the balance sheet, Dollarama has minimal leverage with 2.05x of net leverage on EBITDA, one turn lower than year-end last year. With laddered maturities, a weighted average term of 3.0 years, and an average coupon of 3.6%, the company’s high-grade credit profile and strong ratings from S&P and Moody’s suggest that the balance sheet looks okay.

What To Watch For In Q3'26

Following the quarter, management left FY’26 guidance unchanged on the same-store sales growth figures for between 3-4%, but noted that they’ll likely come in on the high end of the range, considering they already had a strong 1H’26 that already delivered 4.9% growth. On gross margins, management guided toward the high end of the 44.2% to 45.2% gross margin range for the Canadian segment while keeping SG&A guidance steady at 14.2% to 14.7%. To me, this feels appropriately confident yet still prudent, especially because the outlook for the consumer is still uncertain (e.g. with tariffs and interest rates) and it’s possible that discretionary categories could soften further in 2H’26.

When looking out to next quarter, investors should keep a close eye on Canadian same-store sales performance, especially how seasonal categories like back-to-school and Halloween fare, given the fact that we saw pretty flat summer discretionary trends. I'd also watch for updates on store openings (maybe we’ll see another 15–20 stores of the 70-80 target for the full year).

Longer term, the new growth driver will be the international business. Ultimately, I think Dollarama’s expansion is limited here in Canada, and they’ll probably look to find growth in avenues like Mexico and Australia. On the conference call post quarter, management discussed a detailed study of international markets offering both an attractive level of competition and ease of imports. Following two years of discussions with The Reject Shop, Dollarama was attracted by the firm's established footprint, logistical infrastructure, and local team. Looking over the next four years, Dollarama plans to convert and rebrand 100 stores annually to match the Canadian experience, while leveraging its procurement advantage. Most notably, gradual store conversions and the introduction of Dollarama's products remains a priority. Longer term, Australia's margin profile should probably remain below Canada's due to structurally higher labor and lease costs.

Risks

Where Dollarama doesn’t have a lot of control and is exposed is on the macro backdrop. It’s no secret that we’re starting to see some softening retail momentum and eroding consumer confidence. As we head into the company's Q3’26, it’s still a big question mark on whether things will slow down, but if I had to guess, I think holiday sales in Q4’26 will be lower than last year. In October, IPSOS noted that overall confidence was down a point at -6% and there’s a contraction between consumers own personal prospects, contrasted with a waning confidence in the national economy. I’m no expert and have no idea which way it’ll pull, but I do think this caution might simply mean less spending as consumers take a firmer grip on their wallets into year-end.

Another area I do think investors need to watch is the fact that inflation slowed down to 2.2% in October, which probably means we should expect a slowdown on the top line. When we look back just two years ago in 2023, inflation was widely seen as a positive factor for Dollarama as the company’s pricing strategy of introducing items up to $5 meant that it could still be seen as maintaining affordability for consumers and simultaneously attract budget conscious shoppers. Now, that might not be the case as inflation subsides and Dollarama may not have the levers it once did on increasing transaction size, instead looking and growing transactions by increasing its store footprint.

Finally, it’s still early days for the Australian business that Dollarama acquired, and this is by far the largest and most complex project the company has ever undertaken. Practically speaking, this is a 395 store turnaround in a higher-cost labor market with a completely different starting assortment, supply chain, and store format. Management has been clear that the business will not contribute materially to earnings for several years and will instead burn cash on integration and capex during the heavy lifting phase. If we see delays or integration doesn’t go as planned, I’d look to this as being a detractor to the thesis and could push the company’s multiple lower.

Valuation

Dollarama’s stock is getting pricey, and the Street’s target prices reflect that. With an average price target of $199, analysts see just 2.0% upside in addition to the paltry 0.2% dividend. A 2.2% total return period over a year isn’t at all exciting, and so despite the majority ‘buy’ ratings, I’d say that analysts are signaling through their price targets that the stock may be expensive.

Ultimately, Dollarama looks expensive just about anyway you slice it. Against peers like Five Below (FIVE), Dollar General (DG), and Dollar Tree (DLTR), the stock trades at nearly twice the multiple on both a P/E and EV/EBITDA basis, as shown below.

While I’d argue that Dollarama is the best among the group, and trades more akin to a Costco (COST) as was suggested by Bernstein a couple of months ago, the company isn’t closer to a consumer staples company any more than it is a consumer discretionary company and the membership features of a Costco seem to have a bigger pull in terms of customer loyalty. We can also see below that the company trades for a lofty EV/EBITDA multiple that’s expanded quite materially to the upper end of the range.

What's all this worth? Certainly, not worth 42x 2026 earnings or 23x 2030 earnings, in my view. When we look at analyst projections into 2030, most analysts have recognized that the company can't continue putting up 11% growth numbers and that growth should normalize near 7% on the top-line going forward. What's more troubling to me is that even if we assume that Dollarama doubles earnings from its 2025 base to $8.33 in 2030, if the multiple derates from where it is today to a 23x multiple, investors will earn only 0.2% per year, with returns just coming from dividends. Because of this, I think risk is skewed to the downside, as a rerating in Dollarama's multiple is likely to happen unless it can continue putting up mid-teens EPS CAGRs each year.

Bottom Line

Overall, Dollarama remains a high-quality operator with a long runway for growth. But at today’s valuation, I'd say the stock leaves very little room for error. While I’m generally encouraged by the company’s steady execution, disciplined expansion, and the potential of its international initiatives, these growth drivers will take time to fully materialize and carry some integration risk, especially with its new acquisition in Australia. At the same time, softer inflation, a more cautious Canadian consumer, and a stretched multiple all suggest that future returns may be constrained unless Dollarama can continue outperforming expectations. For now, I believe patience is warranted and that investors should avoid the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Targa Resources: Underlying Cash Flow Is Underappreciated
data.description:
Shares of Targa Resources (TRGP) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value. Its relatively low dividend yield (compared to midstream peers) and exposure to the more cyclical NGL portion of the oil & gas sector have been headwinds, and the stock has never really recovered from April losses. That has come despite solid underlying financial results. I last covered shares in August, rating Targa a "Buy," and since then, the stock has gained 7%, suggesting momentum is improving. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if Targa can continue delivering gains for investors.

In the company’s third quarter, reported on Nov. 5, Targa generated $478 million of net income as revenue grew by 8%. Alongside earnings, management said it expects full-year results to come in at the top end of its prior $4.65-$4.85 billion EBITDA guidance range. EBITDA grew 19% from last year to $1.28 billion, and we are seeing the company benefit from strong activity in the key Permian Basin as well as past growth projects coming online.

Gathering and Processing (G&P) operating margin expanded $86 million to $874 million. Permian inlet natural gas volumes were up 11% to 6.6 billion cubic feet, and the company expects low double-digit growth to continue next year. Badlands and coastal volumes were down 2%. Over 80% of volumes now emanate from the Permian, which is the best-performing oil & gas play in the continental United States. With growth capex focused here, that concentration is only likely to grow.

Logistics and Transportation (L&T) profits grew $91 million to $809 million thanks to higher export volumes and NGL growth. NGL transportation volumes were up 23% from last year, while export volumes grew 1%. With several growth projects coming online over the past year, like its Daytona NGL pipeline, the company has more capacity in its system, and with Permian production so strong, Targa is having no difficulty utilizing this extra capacity. Similarly, export volumes are basically running as strong as they can be, given existing capacity, and as expansion projects are completed over the next 18 months, that should support increasing throughput.

Now, the company is spending aggressively to expand capacity. Growth capex should be about $3.3 billion this year with another $250 million of maintenance work. As we can see, existing growth projects are paying off with EBITDA rising and throughput growing. The company also does not plan to slow the pace of expansion, and I expect a similar level of capital spending next year. Most notably, in September, the company announced it would be progressing with its Speedway NFL pipeline in the Permian Basin. This project will have 500kb/d of capacity with the potential to double in capacity. This project will single-handedly increase Targa’s NGL transportation capacity by ~50%. The project will cost $1.6 billion and take two years to complete.

This aggressive spending has led to some concerns that Targa is expanding too much. I am comfortable with its capital allocation. First, while spending aggressively, Targa is funding capex out of cash flow. During the quarter, the company generated $173 million of free cash flow, and year-to-date, free cash flow stands at $491 million. For the year, free cash flow should be ~$700 million.

Targa also has a reasonable balance sheet. The company has $17.4 billion of debt, and leverage ended the quarter at 3.6x. This is consistent with its 3-4x target. Over the next several years, as growth spending slows and free cash flow accelerates, I see the potential for Targa to lower its leverage target towards 3-3.5x, using some incremental free cash flow debt reduction. This, however, is likely ~3 years away.

Aside from financial capacity to support this spending, I do not see Targa as overbuilding beyond potential demand. Permian oil & gas production has been steadily growing for years, and I expect continued growth. In particular, the United States is expanding export capacity of natural gas and gas products, given our abundance of the fuel and its relatively low production cost. Increasing buying commitment has also been a key focus of the Trump Administration’s trade deals, which should continue to support demand. With export prices above domestic prices, this growth provides superior economics, encouraging ongoing capex in the region.

Beyond this, I am optimistic about domestic demand trends. Natural gas is the marginal fuel for most electric utilities. After years of stagnation, electricity demand has been steadily rising. Data centers are growing rapidly, and they will be an incremental driver of electricity demand. All else equal, this incremental usage should lead to increased demand for natural gas, providing additional support to Targa’s growth ambitions.

Now, because of its large capex program, Targa pays a lower dividend than most peers. Shares currently have a 2.1% dividend yield. That said, in its Q3 earnings release, Targa said it anticipates raising its dividend by 25% to $5 in 2026. The board will still need to declare these dividends, but I would be surprised to see a deviation from this announcement. That gives shares a prospective 2.8% dividend yield. While the company has a lower dividend yield than peers, it has been aggressively growing its payout post-COVID. Its 5-year dividend growth rate is 25%.

Targa has been in the midst of a large growth capex program, leading it to run a lower dividend payout, instead doing more buybacks (which are more flexible). The company bought another $156 million of stock in Q3. Its share count is down about 1.8% over the past year, thanks to buybacks. As growth capex projects are completed, this will free up cash flow to return to investors via dividends. Once Targa completes its Speedway and LPG export spending, we should see growth capex decline significantly, enabling a significant inflection in free cash flow. As such, I expect 20+% annual dividend growth to continue through 2028 at least. While its starting dividend yield is lower, its dividend growth is excellent. Plus, combined with buybacks, its capital return yield is still around 4% this year.

Back in August, I was targeting $3.6-$3.7 billion of DCF and $700-$800 million of free cash flow. While I view an 8% DCF yield as appropriate, I believed its lower dividend yield would limit near-term upside. This drove my $190 target. I expect results this year to be in this range. Next year, I see the company generating at least $5.25 billion of EBITDA, supporting about $4 billion of distributable cash flow and at least $750 million of capex, depending on growth spending.

This gives the company over $18 of forward distributable cash flow per share, giving shares a 10+% DCF yield. I continue to view this as very attractive, given Targa’s prime positioning in the Permian and meaningful demand tailwinds. Over time, more of this DCF will be allocated to shareholders as capex slows, and at this valuation, no credit, in my view, is being given for pending contributions from its ongoing growth projects. I view shares as a "Buy." I see underlying fair value as reaching $225+ over the next year, though I still expect its lower dividend yield to limit the ability to reach this. I view $200 as a more reasonable 12-month price target, and that leaves me rating Targa a “Buy.”

data.title: DoorDash: Moving Beyond Food To Become The Future Of Local Commerce (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
DoorDash, Inc. (DASH) is one of the seemingly few pandemic winners which has remained a winner even after the pandemic. The company has proven that food delivery is not a transient trend and offers a clear value proposition to the entire ecosystem. I am impressed with the company’s ability to sustain rapid top-line growth in addition to pushing into GAAP profitability. The stock has sold off recently due to management’s guide for increased investments into its platform, but I see a long-term secular growth runway as the company aims to deliver everything beyond food. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

DASH Stock Price

I last covered DASH in March, where I downgraded the stock to a neutral rating on account of the valuation. The stock has underperformed the broader market by around 17% since.

The stock has finally taken a breather in light of elevated investment activity, creating an attractive buying opportunity in a long-term winner.

DASH Stock Key Metrics

DASH is primarily known as a food delivery company, but management has made it clear that they are aiming to help facilitate the delivery of everything retail. The expansion of their addressable market has helped the company to sustain aggressive top-line growth even many years following the pandemic. The latest quarter saw the company deliver 25% YoY growth in marketplace gross order volume (‘GOV’) to $25.0 billion, crushing guidance of between $24.2 billion to $24.7 billion. The company has notably seen marketplace GOV growth accelerate sequentially for many consecutive quarters. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $754 million, squarely in between guidance of $680 million to $780 million. I suspect that the fact that it did not exceed the high end of guidance was viewed as the first red flag by investors.

The second and apparently more important red flag was management’s commentary that they intend to invest “several hundred million dollars more” into the platform next year. Growth companies often have to strike a balance between growth and profitability, and it looks like the pendulum will swing more towards the former in the near future.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up to $29.5 billion in marketplace GOV in the fourth quarter, representing 38.5% YoY growth.

I note that this will be the first quarter with a near-full contribution from their acquisition of Deliveroo, which explains the large acceleration in growth.

The board of directors had previously authorized a share repurchase program of up to $5 billion of stock back in February, but the company has not made use of that program year to date. DASH ended the quarter with $5.1 billion of cash and investments versus $2.7 billion of debt, which along with the positive GAAP profitability, represents a strong net cash balance sheet.

On the conference call, management gave more clarity on their investment plans, noting that adjusted EBITDA margins might be “up slightly compared to 2025.” That would differ substantially from the rapid margin expansion that the company has delivered in recent years. Management called out their main priority as “building a new global tech platform” to manage their main business units in DoorDash, Deliveroo, and Wolt. My opinion is that this investment makes sense and may set the company up for rapid margin expansion potential beyond 2026 as it benefits from company synergies. Management reiterated their vision to “turn every physical retailer into an omni-channel player.” As a food delivery consumer, I have seen the competitive battle between DASH and Uber Eats (UBER). The fight for the delivery of retail items is looking like a battle for market share dominance, similar to what we previously saw in ridesharing between UBER and Lyft (LYFT).

Is DASH Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At first glance, DASH might not look buyable at all, with the stock trading at just around 32x forward earnings.

There are two key points here. The first is that the company is projected to sustain double-digit top-line growth over the coming years. I see continued upside in food delivery, but more importantly, consistent secular upside as the company reaches out to everything other than restaurants.

The second point is the business model quality. Those who have never used the product might view this to be the kind of luxury that goes away in a recession. As a loyal user of DoorDash, I am strongly of the opposite opinion. DASH offers a clear value proposition to all parties as it boosts business for restaurants, increases convenience for customers, and creates jobs for the “dashers.” Food delivery (and delivery of everything) is a business that arguably should have always existed, but it just hadn’t existed in earnest until this decade due to there not being the technology to match orders at scale. The key question is whether customers might prove promiscuous with their business, perhaps switching between DASH and UBER or other competitors based on pricing. I am a customer of both DASH and UBER, and I have the opinion that the fact that UBER has its mobility product in addition to delivery has not created sufficient reason to ditch my DASH services. This is an anecdotal datapoint, but DASH excels in terms of customer service, whereas it is very difficult (and frustrating) to even begin to troubleshoot any issues on UBER. I note that this sentiment appears to be shared by others as well.

Based on consensus estimates for around 16% to 18% forward growth (I note that 2026 consensus estimates are likely impacted by the acquisition of Deliveroo), the stock trades at around a 1.5x to 2.0x price to earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). That PEG ratio declines if one believes that DASH can realize operating leverage over the long term, which I do. It is difficult to make a call for multiple expansion from here (that would need the stock to trade under a 1.2x PEG ratio), but I can see the stock delivering outsized returns from primarily the ongoing annual growth and also the GAAP earnings yield.

DASH Stock Risks

DASH is not cheap here, even after the recent underperformance. I currently view the business model as being of high quality, but it bears reminding that these “gig” companies had previously been widely seen as poster children of the 2021 bubble in tech stocks. It is possible that DASH sees its multiple compress, perhaps if it enters an aggressive pricing war with UBER or others. It is possible that Amazon (AMZN) eventually enters the competitive environment in a meaningful way. It is possible that local governments enact laws forcing the company to increase its driver compensation or operational cost structure.

DASH Stock Conclusion

Many pandemic winners saw their growth rates disappear following the pandemic. DASH bucked that trend and has continued to post strong double-digit growth even many years after. The company is now profitable on a GAAP basis, and I expect more margin expansion over the long term. I expect the company to see success in creating a new delivery market beyond restaurants. DASH has many of the traits that I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.
